narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes a solid Year 1 school decile of 6, which is slightly above average, and a Year 3 decile of 5, just below the typical level, along with a lower-middle family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 2.20, an LSAT score of 31, and plans to attend full-time like most students. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 60% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test—a threshold that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA, well below the average of 3.21, signaling to the model a foundational academic hurdle that often correlates with bar struggles. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the typical 36.7, was the next strongest influence in the same concerning direction, highlighting a key standardized measure where you fell short of peers. On a brighter note, your Year 3 decile actually nudged the prediction toward success, as it reflects resilience in later schooling despite the dip. Your stronger Year 1 decile and full-time commitment provided smaller positive pulls, while family income had minimal sway. Compared to typical students who pass, your GPA and LSAT stand out as notably lower, outweighing those academic gains in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergrad record with post-bac courses or a master's, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for underrepresented students to build bar-ready skills. You're capable of rewriting this story; persistence like yours changes outcomes every day. Keep pushing—we believe in you.
